lessons.title: States of Matter and Particle Theory

content.body: All matter is made of tiny particles — atoms, molecules, or ions. The arrangement and movement of these particles determines whether a substance is solid, liquid, or gas. This is the Kinetic Theory of Matter.

SOLID:
• Particles closely packed in regular arrangement
• Particles vibrate in fixed positions
• Fixed shape and volume
• Cannot flow
• High density

LIQUID:
• Particles close together but can move past each other
• Particles slide and flow
• Fixed volume but no fixed shape
• Can flow, takes shape of container

GAS:
• Particles far apart, random motion
• Particles move very fast in all directions
• No fixed shape or volume
• Low density, can be compressed

content.title: Changes of State
content.body:
Melting: solid → liquid (heating)
Freezing: liquid → solid (cooling)
Evaporation/Boiling: liquid → gas (heating)
Condensation: gas → liquid (cooling)
Sublimation: solid → gas directly (e.g. dry ice, iodine)
Deposition: gas → solid directly

Melting point and boiling point are fixed temperatures for pure substances at standard pressure. During melting and boiling, temperature stays constant — the energy is used to overcome forces between particles, not to increase temperature.

questions.question: In which state of matter do particles have the most energy?
questions.explanation: Gas particles move fastest and have the highest kinetic energy.

questions.question: What happens to particles during melting?
options: They gain energy and move more freely
They move into a regular arrangement
questions.answer: They gain energy and move more freely
questions.explanation: During melting, particles gain enough energy to break from fixed positions and move past each other.

questions.question: Which state of matter has a fixed volume but no fixed shape?
questions.explanation: Liquids have fixed volume (particles close together) but no fixed shape (particles can flow).

questions.question: Sublimation is when:
options: A solid changes directly to gas
questions.answer: A solid changes directly to gas
questions.explanation: Sublimation is the direct change from solid to gas, skipping the liquid state (e.g. iodine, carbon dioxide).

questions.question: Why does temperature stay constant during boiling?
options: The fire is not hot enough
Energy is used to overcome intermolecular forces, not raise temperature
All particles are already moving fast
questions.answer: Energy is used to overcome intermolecular forces, not raise temperature
questions.explanation: At boiling point, all added energy breaks forces between liquid particles (latent heat of vaporisation) — temperature doesn't rise until all liquid has vaporised.

questions.question: Condensation is the change from:
questions.explanation: Condensation: gas → liquid. This occurs when a gas cools and loses energy.

questions.question: Particles in a solid are:
options: Moving very fast in straight lines
questions.explanation: Solid particles are packed closely in an ordered/regular arrangement and vibrate around fixed positions.

questions.question: Which state of matter is most easily compressed?
questions.explanation: Gas particles are far apart with lots of space between them, so gases can be compressed easily.

questions.question: When water freezes, what change of state occurs?
questions.explanation: Freezing: liquid → solid. Water (liquid) becomes ice (solid) when cooled to 0°C.

questions.question: The melting point of a substance is:
options: The same as its boiling point
questions.explanation: Each pure substance has its own fixed melting point — this is used to identify substances.
